title: The gate that caught a renamed column before prod did

steps.narration: 10:42. A commit lands on main: a1c9f04 — "silver_orders: tidy column names". It renames fraud_score to risk_score in the silver transform, and updates the one unit-test fixture to match. CI fires the pipeline: lint → unit → integration → data-quality gate → deploy(staging). Each stage is a required check; a red stage blocks promotion.

state.note: commit a1c9f04 · branch main · pipeline queued · deploy target = staging (prod is gated behind staging)

steps.narration: Stage 1 — lint. ruff and black --check run in 11 seconds across 42 files: style, unused imports, formatting. The rename is perfectly well-formatted Python, so lint has nothing to say. Green. This is the cheapest gate and it only ever catches surface problems — it was never going to catch this one.

state.note: 42 files clean · 0 findings — lint validates form, not behavior

steps.narration: Stage 2 — unit tests with chispa-style DataFrame assertions. 47 tests build tiny in-memory DataFrames and compare them with assert_df_equality. They pass in 38 seconds — including the fraud-queue test, because the same commit updated that fixture to say risk_score. The test and the code agree with each other. They just no longer agree with the rest of the platform.

state.note: 47 passed, 0 failed — a green unit suite proves internal consistency, not contract compliance

steps.narration: Stage 3 — integration on a golden sample. The real pipeline runs end-to-end against a frozen 240-order NimbusMart fixture (seed 42), writing Silver and Gold to a scratch namespace. It completes in 96 seconds with a zero exit code. No exception is thrown: renaming a column is a perfectly successful operation. The job is green and the output is wrong.

state.note: exit 0 · Silver + Gold written to scratch · no crash — 'it ran' is not 'it is correct'

steps.narration: Stage 4 — the data-quality gate. It does not run the code; it inspects the code's output against a contract. Two checks matter here: a schema contract (the Gold fraud_review_queue must expose exactly these columns and types) and a row expectation (on the golden sample, exactly 43 orders sit at or above FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD). It starts evaluating both.

state.note: gate = declared expectations on output: schema contract + row-count bounds + null/uniqueness rules

steps.narration: The gate fails. The schema-contract check reports: fraud_review_queue is missing required column fraud_score; found unexpected column risk_score. And the row expectation collapsed — the downstream join keys on fraud_score, which no longer exists, so the queue came out with 0 rows instead of 43. Two red assertions, 9 seconds in.

state.note: FAIL · required column fraud_score absent (renamed to risk_score) · review-queue rows 0 ≠ 43

steps.narration: Promotion is blocked. The deploy stage never starts — a required check is red, so the commit cannot reach staging, let alone prod. CI posts the failure back on the commit with the two failing expectations. The author sees it in ~90 seconds of pushing, at their desk, with full context — the cheapest possible place to learn the column is load-bearing.

state.note: deploy skipped — upstream required check failed · commit a1c9f04 marked not-promotable

steps.narration: Play the counterfactual — no data-quality gate. Lint, unit, and integration were all green, so the commit ships to staging and, on the next promotion, to prod. Tonight's nightly DAG runs the fraud-review task: it produces 0 flagged orders. Every fraudulent order at or above FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD auto-fulfills. Nobody is paged, because nothing failed — the queue is simply, silently empty.

bars.label: orders flagged for review (no gate, in prod)

state.note: a green pipeline with no output contract ships correctness bugs straight to prod — silently

steps.narration: The fix is a two-line diff: keep the column name fraud_score (or update every downstream consumer and the schema contract in the same PR). Re-push as b0e7712. The pipeline reruns: lint 11s, unit 38s, integration 95s, gate green — 43 rows, schema matches. Deploy to staging proceeds. Same five stages, one honest green.

state.note: commit b0e7712 · all 5 stages green · review queue = 43 rows · promoted to staging

steps.narration: The lesson is where the layers stop each other. Lint guards form; unit tests guard internal logic; integration guards that it runs; only the data-quality gate guards that the output still means what consumers depend on. Unit tests moved with the bug — the gate could not, because it is pinned to the contract, not to the code. Gate your pipelines on their output, in dev-then-staging-then-prod, and prod stops being where you discover the column mattered.

state.note:
the CI/CD ladder for data: lint → unit(chispa) → integration(sample) → data-quality gate → promote
tests can drift with the code; a contract-pinned gate is what catches the change the tests were edited to allow
